text.content: MINI PRD: P44 — FluidFlow Relief Valve Sizing
Status: Plan
Batch: 5 | Priority: P44 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2000-3000
What This Article Does Now

Covers relief valve sizing capabilities in FluidFlow. Likely explains standards compliance (API RP 520, ISO 4126-1). May lack practical guidance on sizing scenarios, selection criteria, and validation against vendor software.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Provide comprehensive guide to relief valve sizing in FluidFlow covering liquid, gas, and two-phase scenarios. Must explain WHAT standards FluidFlow uses (API RP 520, ISO 4126-1), HOW to set up sizing cases (overpressure scenarios, set pressure configurations, discharge conditions), WHEN to use each standard, and HOW to validate results. Include practical examples for common scenarios (fire case, blocked outlet, runaway reaction).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering challenge (sizing relief devices for process safety), what the reader will learn, name FluidFlow's relief valve sizing capabilities and standards compliance.
**Add "Standards Overview" section**: Brief explanation of API RP 520 and ISO 4126-1, when each applies (API for oil/gas/chemical, ISO more general), key differences in methodology if any.

**Add "Relief Scenarios" section**: Explain three set pressure configurations (10% MAWP sole device, 16% MAWP multiple device, 21% MAWP fire case), when to use each, how FluidFlow handles each scenario.
**Add step-by-step sizing procedures for each fluid type**:
**Liquid Relief**: Incompressible flow, sizing based on flow coefficient, backpressure effects
**Gas Relief**: Compressible flow, critical/subcritical flow, gas properties at relieving conditions
**Two-Phase Relief**: Most complex, homogeneous equilibrium model, omega method if applicable, choked flow considerations

**Add "Setting Up a Relief Valve Calculation" section**: Numbered steps (define relieving scenario, select standard, configure set pressure, specify discharge conditions, run calculation, review orifice size and vendor selection).
**Add "Rupture Disc Sizing" section**: How FluidFlow handles rupture discs (Kd values for different types: sharp edge, bellmouth, in-projecting, API, liquid), when to use vs relief valve.
**Add validation and verification section**: How to check results (compare to hand calculations, cross-check with vendor software if available, validate assumptions like adiabatic/isothermal).
**Add practical examples**: Include 2-3 scenarios (e.g., blocked outlet on pump discharge, fire case on storage tank, runaway reaction with two-phase flow).

**Add Best Practices section**: Common mistakes (wrong set pressure configuration, ignoring backpressure, incorrect fluid properties at relieving conditions, overlooking two-phase effects), when to consult process safety specialist.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting accurate relief valve sizing to process safety, regulatory compliance, and avoiding under-protection or costly over-sizing.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Relief Valve](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#relief-valve) — in opening
[Set Pressure](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#set-pressure) — in scenarios section
[MAWP](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#mawp) — in scenarios section
[Choked Flow](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#choked-flow) — in gas/two-phase sections
[Backpressure](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#backpressure) — in liquid section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page)
SIDEWAYS: P41 Two-Phase Choked Flow, P13 Quality Assurance & Standards, P11 Designer Handbook
DOWN (glossary): Relief Valve, Set Pressure, MAWP, Choked Flow, Backpressure
BLOG: Process safety or relief valve articles if available
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact/ (for complex safety-critical applications)
Selling Message

1 — "40+ years of proven accuracy" — weave into validation section: FluidFlow's relief valve sizing methods are validated against decades of industry use and published test data, providing confidence for safety-critical applications. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Relief Valve sizing (API RP 520, ISO 4126-1 standards)
All modules (Liquid, Gas, Two-Phase relief scenarios)
Set pressure configurations (10%, 16%, 21% MAWP)
Rupture disc sizing (Kd values for 5 types)
Vendor database (relief valve manufacturers)
Auto-sizing capabilities
Choked flow detection (Gas and Two-Phase)
MINI PRD: P45 — FluidFlow Fire Systems
Status: Plan
Batch: 5 | Priority: P45 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 2000-3000
What This Article Does Now

Covers FluidFlow's capabilities for fire protection systems. Likely mentions sprinkler modeling and standards. May lack depth on NFPA compliance, hydraulic calculations methodology, and practical design workflows.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Provide comprehensive guide to modeling fire protection systems (sprinklers, standpipes, hydrants) in FluidFlow. Must explain WHAT fire system elements FluidFlow supports (sprinkler heads, atmospheric discharge ends, hydrants, fire pumps), HOW to model hydraulically calculated systems vs pre-engineered systems, WHICH standards/codes are addressed (NFPA 13, NFPA 14, NFPA 20 if applicable), and HOW to generate compliant documentation.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the engineering challenge (designing code-compliant fire protection systems with accurate hydraulic calculations), what the reader will learn, name FluidFlow's fire system modeling capabilities.

**Add "Fire System Elements" section**: List specific elements available (sprinkler heads with K-factors, atmospheric discharge boundaries, fire hydrants, fire pumps, standpipe connections). Explain how each is represented in FluidFlow.

**Add "Standards and Compliance" section**: Explain which NFPA standards are relevant (NFPA 13 for sprinklers, NFPA 14 for standpipes, possibly NFPA 20 for fire pumps). Clarify what FluidFlow calculates (hydraulics) vs what engineer must verify (code compliance, spacing, coverage).

**Add "Hydraulic Calculation Methods" section**: Explain Hazen-Williams equation (standard for fire protection), pressure requirements at remote sprinkler head, accounting for elevation changes, friction losses through fittings.

**Add step-by-step design workflow**: Clear numbered steps (define system layout, place sprinkler heads with K-factors, configure discharge pressure requirements, model fire pump if needed, run hydraulic calculation, generate results for AHJ submittal).
**Add "Sprinkler Head Modeling" section**: How to enter K-factors, pressure-flow relationship, atmospheric discharge boundary setup.

**Add "Results and Documentation" section**: What outputs FluidFlow provides for fire system submittals (pressure at each node, flow through each branch, pump performance, velocity checks), configuring reports for AHJ (Authority Having Jurisdiction) review.
**Add practical examples**: Include 1-2 scenarios (e.g., light hazard office building sprinkler system, ordinary hazard warehouse with ESFR sprinklers).

**Add Best Practices section**: Common mistakes (wrong Hazen-Williams C-factor, ignoring elevation properly, undersizing fire pump, not accounting for simultaneous sprinkler operation areas), validation checks.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting accurate fire system hydraulics to life safety, code compliance, and successful AHJ approvals.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
**Add disclaimer if appropriate**: Note that FluidFlow performs hydraulic calculations; engineer is responsible for ensuring code compliance, coverage, spacing, and all other NFPA requirements.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Hazen-Williams](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#hazen-williams) — in calculation methods section
[K-Factor](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#k-factor) — in sprinkler head section
[Head](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#head) — in pressure requirements section
[Duty Point](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#duty-point) — in fire pump section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page, Liquid module)
SIDEWAYS: P13 Quality Assurance & Standards, P38 Modeling a Centrifugal Pump (fire pumps), P11 Designer Handbook
DOWN (glossary): Hazen-Williams, K-Factor, Head, Duty Point
BLOG: Fire protection articles if available
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact/ (for complex fire system applications)
Selling Message

1 — "40+ years of proven accuracy" — weave into standards section: FluidFlow has been used for fire protection hydraulic calculations across thousands of installations worldwide, with proven accuracy meeting AHJ requirements. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Liquid Module (fire systems = water flow)
Hazen-Williams correlation (standard for fire protection)
Atmospheric discharge boundaries (sprinkler heads)
Fire hydrant element (if available in database)
Pump modeling (fire pumps)
Elevation handling (critical for multi-story buildings)
Valve database (control valves, check valves in fire systems)
MINI PRD: P46 — FluidFlow Release History
Status: Plan
Batch: 5 | Priority: P46 | Type: Reference | Word Target: 1500-2500
What This Article Does Now
Lists FluidFlow version history and features. Likely a chronological list of releases. May lack organization by feature category, search/navigation aids, or highlights of major milestones.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Provide well-organized reference for FluidFlow version history, highlighting major feature additions, module releases, and capability enhancements over 40+ year history. Must be scannable (version table), searchable (clear headings), and highlight key milestones (module launches, standards added, database expansions).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (comprehensive version history), how to use this reference (find when features were added, understand software evolution), current version (v3.54 as of spec sheet).

**Add "Version Highlights" section at top**: Summary table of major releases with key features (e.g., v3.54: Hydrogen NIST density; v3.52: Multi-Calc functionality; earlier: Two-Phase module launch, Slurry module launch, etc.). This provides quick navigation before full chronological list.
**Add "How to Use This Reference" section**: Explain organization (chronological, newest first), how to find when a feature was added, link to download page for latest version.

**Organize full history by version**: Clear version number headings (e.g., "### Version 3.54 — Released [Date]"), bulleted feature lists under each version, grouped by category where possible (New Features, Enhancements, Bug Fixes).
**Add milestone callouts**: Highlight major events (e.g., "Two-Phase Module Introduced", "1,000th Fluid Added to Database", "API RP 520 Compliance Added") with bold or special formatting.
**Add "Looking Forward" section at bottom**: Brief mention of upcoming features if publicly discussed (e.g., "FlowDesigner cloud platform launching 2026" from context, if appropriate).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword.
**Ensure full version history is included**: If current content has partial history, note "[EXPAND with complete version history]" for content writer to fill from release notes archive.
Glossary Links (first use only)
NONE — This is a reference document, glossary links not needed.
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page)
SIDEWAYS: P43 FluidFlow Database (related reference doc), P47 Scripting Handbook (another reference doc)
CTA: Pricing — https://fluidflowinfo.com/pricing/ (to upgrade to latest version)
Selling Message

1 — "40+ years of proven accuracy" — weave into opening: FluidFlow's 40+ year development history demonstrates continuous innovation and commitment to accuracy, with thousands of installations worldwide. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Complete version history (extract from release notes if available)
Current version: v3.54

Major milestones: Module launches (Liquid, Gas, Two-Phase, Slurry, Scripting), database expansions (1,283 fluids), standards additions (API RP 520, ISO 4126-1, ISO 5167), recent enhancements (Hydrogen NIST density v3.53, Multi-Calc v3.52, PCF enhancements)
MINI PRD: P47 — FluidFlow Scripting Handbook
Status: Plan
Batch: 5 | Priority: P47 | Type: Deep-dive | Word Target: 3000-5000
What This Article Does Now
Likely technical reference for FluidFlow Scripting Module. May cover scripting syntax, API reference, example scripts. Could be extensive technical documentation requiring major enhancement.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as comprehensive technical reference and tutorial for FluidFlow Scripting Module. Must explain WHAT the Scripting Module enables (light dynamic analysis, multi-calc, back-calc, custom calculations), HOW to write scripts (syntax, API reference, accessing model elements), and PROVIDE practical examples for common use cases. This is a deep-dive technical resource for advanced users.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (scripting enables custom calculations and dynamic analysis), who this is for (advanced users comfortable with scripting), what the reader will learn.

**Add "Scripting Module Overview" section**: Explain three main capabilities: Light Dynamic Analysis, Multi-Calc functionality, Back Calc Input Tool (reverse calculations). Provide 2-3 sentence explanation of each.
**Add "Getting Started with Scripting" section**: Prerequisites (FluidFlow license with Scripting Module), accessing scripting interface, basic script structure, running first script.

**Add "Scripting Language Reference" section**: Document syntax, data types, control structures, built-in functions. If this is extensive, consider organizing as subsections or linking to external API docs.
**Add "Accessing Model Elements" section**: How to reference nodes, pipes, equipment in scripts, reading/writing properties, accessing calculation results.
**Add "Common Use Cases" section with examples**:
**Multi-Calc**: Running parametric studies (vary pipe diameter, pump speed, etc. and collect results)
**Back-Calc**: Solving for unknown inputs to meet target outputs
**Light Dynamic Analysis**: Time-stepping simulations for slowly-varying conditions (NOT full transient — clarify distinction from FlowDesigner)
**Add "Best Practices" section**: Script organization, error handling, commenting code, validation checks, performance considerations.
**Add "Limitations" section**: Clarify what Scripting Module does NOT do (full transient analysis = FlowDesigner, CFD analysis, etc.).
**Add "Troubleshooting" section**: Common errors, debugging techniques, where to get help.
**Add "Example Scripts Library" section**: Link to or provide 3-5 complete example scripts with explanations (e.g., pump sequencing analysis, pipe diameter optimization, system curve generation).
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting Scripting Module to solving custom engineering problems that go beyond standard steady-state analysis.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
**Note if content needs technical review**: Flag "[REQUIRES TECHNICAL REVIEW OF API SYNTAX]" if script examples need validation by FluidFlow development team.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Steady-State](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#steady-state) — in limitations section
[Transient](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#transient) — in limitations section (clarify this is FlowDesigner, not Scripting Module)
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page, Scripting Module section)
SIDEWAYS: P35 Back Calc Input Tool, P11 Designer Handbook, P46 Release History
DOWN (glossary): Steady-State, Transient
BLOG: Advanced techniques articles if available
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact/ (for scripting support or training)
Selling Message
NONE — This is a technical reference. Avoid marketing language; focus on clear documentation.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Scripting Module (Light Dynamic Analysis, Multi-Calc, Back Calc Input Tool)
Scripting API (document structure, syntax, available functions)
Example scripts (if library available)
Limitations: Full transient = FlowDesigner (not available yet), Scripting = "light" dynamic analysis only
MINI PRD: P48 — FluidFlow Mining White Paper
Status: Plan
Batch: 5 | Priority: P48 | Type: Industry Solution | Word Target: 3000-4000
What This Article Does Now
Likely white paper on FluidFlow for mining applications. May cover slurry transport, tailings, paste backfill. Could be technical marketing content or case study collection.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as comprehensive industry solution guide for mining and minerals processing applications. Must explain WHAT FluidFlow solves in mining (slurry transport, tailings disposal, paste backfill, mineral concentrate pipelines, mine dewatering), HOW FluidFlow addresses unique mining challenges (settling slurries, high solids concentrations, deposition velocity, pump wear), and WHY FluidFlow is suited for mining (industry-specific correlations, deposition velocity models, centrifugal pump derating for solids).
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the industry challenge (reliable, efficient slurry transport in mining operations), what the reader will learn, position FluidFlow as industry choice for mining applications.

**Add "Mining Industry Challenges" section**: Brief overview of unique hydraulic challenges in mining (high solids concentrations, abrasive particles, deposition and blockage risks, pump wear and selection, long-distance transport, variable ore properties).
**Add "FluidFlow Capabilities for Mining" section**: Structured overview of relevant features:
**Slurry Module**: 8 settling slurry correlations (Vsm, V50, 4CM, Durand, Wasp, WASC, Sellgren-Wilson, Liu Dezhong), vertical piping methods
**Deposition Velocity**: 3 maximum deposition velocity methods (WASC, particle size function, GIW VSCALC), critical velocity calculations
**Pump Performance**: 5 centrifugal pump derating methods for solids (RP King, HI Guidelines, ANSI 2021, GIW 4CM, Fixed Deration)
**Non-Newtonian Behavior**: 4 rheology models for paste backfill and thickened tailings
**Add "Common Mining Applications" section**: Cover 4-5 specific use cases with brief explanations:
Mineral concentrate pipelines (long-distance transport)
Tailings disposal systems (deposition velocity critical)
Paste backfill systems (high yield stress, non-Newtonian)
Mine dewatering (multiphase if water + air/gas)
Process plant slurry circuits (cyclones, thickeners)

**Add "Design Workflow for Mining Systems" section**: Step-by-step approach (characterize slurry properties, select correlation, determine deposition velocity, size piping for safe velocity margin, select and derate pumps, validate design).

**Add "Case Study or Example" section**: Provide 1-2 detailed examples with system parameters, design decisions, and results. If actual case studies not available, create representative examples based on typical mining scenarios.

**Add "Best Practices for Mining Applications" section**: Common mistakes (ignoring deposition velocity, undersizing for wear/degradation, not accounting for variable ore properties, inadequate pump selection), validation approaches (pilot testing, comparison to existing operations).

**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting FluidFlow mining capabilities to operational reliability, reduced downtime, and optimized capital costs in challenging slurry transport applications.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
**Add references section**: If citing published methods or case studies, include proper citations.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Slurry](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#slurry) — in opening
[Deposition Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#deposition-velocity) — in challenges section
[Non-Newtonian](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#non-newtonian) — in paste backfill section
[Critical Velocity](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#critical-velocity) — in deposition section
[Rheology](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#rheology) — in paste backfill section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page, Slurry module)
SIDEWAYS: P11 Designer Handbook (if covers slurry), P13 Quality Assurance & Standards, P38 Modeling a Centrifugal Pump (slurry pump derating)
DOWN (glossary): Slurry, Deposition Velocity, Non-Newtonian, Critical Velocity, Rheology
BLOG: Mining or slurry transport articles if available
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact/ (for mining project support)
Selling Message

1 — "40+ years of proven accuracy" — weave into opening: FluidFlow has been the industry choice for mining slurry system design for decades, with installations across global mining operations. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
Slurry Module (all capabilities)
8 settling slurry friction loss correlations
3 maximum deposition velocity methods
Characteristic velocity calculations (Oroskar-Turian, Schiller-Herbich, Wilson-GIW, Thomas, etc.)
5 centrifugal pump derating methods
4 rheology models (for paste backfill)
Vertical piping methods (3 options)
Non-Newtonian friction loss methods (Darby, Chilton-Stainsby, Converted Power Law)
11+ solid materials database
MINI PRD: P49 — PCF File Import Guide
Status: Plan
Batch: 5 | Priority: P49 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now

Explains how to import PCF (Piping Component File) format into FluidFlow. Likely covers basic import steps. May lack detail on PCF format specifics, data mapping, troubleshooting import errors, and validation of imported models.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Provide step-by-step guide for importing PCF files into FluidFlow, addressing format requirements, data mapping, and validation. Must explain WHAT PCF format is (isometric piping data from CAD/design systems), WHY import is valuable (leverage existing design data, avoid manual re-entry), HOW to prepare PCF files, HOW to import into FluidFlow, and HOW to validate imported model accuracy.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (importing piping isometric data from CAD/design systems), what the reader will accomplish, name FluidFlow's PCF import capability.

**Add "What is PCF Format?" section**: Brief explanation (2-3 sentences) — PCF is ASCII-based piping isometric data format, commonly exported from CAD systems (PDMS, E3D, SmartPlant, etc.), contains pipe routing, diameters, fittings, equipment.

**Add "Before You Import" section**: Prerequisites (PCF file from CAD system, FluidFlow license, understanding of what PCF does/doesn't contain — e.g., may lack fluid properties, operating conditions, equipment curves).

**Add step-by-step import procedure**: Clear numbered steps (obtain PCF file, open FluidFlow, select Import PCF, navigate to file, configure import options if available, review import log, validate imported model).

**Add "PCF Data Mapping" section**: Explain what PCF data becomes in FluidFlow (pipe segments → Pipe elements, bends/tees → Fittings, valves → Valve elements, equipment → generic junctions requiring configuration). Note what must be added post-import (fluid properties, boundary conditions, equipment details like pump curves).

**Add "Validation and Completion" section**: Mandatory post-import checks (verify connectivity, check pipe diameters match isometric, confirm elevations if 3D data included, add missing equipment details, set fluid properties and boundary conditions, run calculation to check for errors).

**Add "Troubleshooting Import Errors" section**: Common problems (malformed PCF file, unsupported elements, missing data, duplicate components) and solutions (check PCF file syntax, contact CAD system support, manually correct in FluidFlow).

**Add "Best Practices" section**: When PCF import is appropriate (large existing designs, integration with CAD workflow), when manual entry is better (small systems, conceptual design), PCF file quality checks.
**Add "So What?" closing**: Bold italic statement connecting PCF import to faster project delivery by leveraging existing design data and avoiding error-prone manual re-entry.
**Add glossary links on first use** (see below).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
[Isometric](https://fluidflowinfo.com/glossary/#isometric) — in PCF explanation section
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page)
SIDEWAYS: P11 Designer Handbook (workflow context), P02 Build Your First Network (for post-import model completion)
DOWN (glossary): Isometric
BLOG: CAD integration or workflow articles if available
CTA: Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message

3 — "Fastest path from concept to confident design" — weave into opening: PCF import eliminates manual re-entry of piping layouts, accelerating analysis of existing designs. Do not repeat as separate pitch.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
PCF import capability (mentioned in spec sheet: "PCF enhancements for pipe diameter and length properties")
Data management features (JSON format support, FlowDesigner compatibility)
All modules (PCF import works across system types)
MINI PRD: P50 — License Manager in FluidFlow
Status: Plan
Batch: 5 | Priority: P50 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 1000-1500
What This Article Does Now
Explains FluidFlow License Manager functionality. Likely covers installation, activation, network licensing. May lack troubleshooting steps and multi-user management guidance.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Provide comprehensive guide to FluidFlow License Manager covering installation, activation, network licensing, and troubleshooting. Must explain WHAT license types are available (annual, quarterly, perpetual, named user), HOW to install and activate licenses (manual and automatic methods), HOW to set up network licensing for multi-user environments, and HOW to troubleshoot common license issues.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the purpose (managing FluidFlow licenses for single or multi-user installations), what the reader will accomplish, who needs this guide (IT administrators, license administrators, individual users).
**Add "License Types Overview" section**: Brief explanation of available license types:
Annual Lease (most common, includes SUM)
Quarterly Lease
Perpetual (available from 2 seats upwards)
Named User
Trial (14 days, requires online login)
**Add "Installation Workflow" section**: High-level overview (install FluidFlow software → install License Manager → activate license → verify functionality).

**Add "Single-User License Activation" section**: Step-by-step for individual installations (launch License Manager, select manual or automatic activation, enter license key, complete activation, verify in FluidFlow).

**Add "Network License Setup" section**: Step-by-step for multi-user environments (install License Manager on server, configure network settings, activate server license, install FluidFlow on client workstations, point clients to license server, verify connectivity). Note prerequisites (network connectivity, firewall rules, ports).

**Add "Manual vs Automatic Activation" section**: Explain difference (automatic requires internet connection, manual for air-gapped systems), when to use each, steps for manual activation (generate request file, submit to FluidFlow support, receive activation file, import).
**Add "Troubleshooting Common Issues" section**: Address typical problems:
License not found (check License Manager running, verify network path)
Activation failed (check internet connection for automatic, verify license key for manual)
All seats in use (check active users, release unused seats, consider additional licenses)
Version mismatch (license version vs software version compatibility)
**Add "License Maintenance" section**: Renewing annual licenses, upgrading from trial to full license, adding seats, transferring licenses.
**Add "Best Practices" section**: Network license server placement (reliable machine, backup), monitoring license usage, planning for renewals.
**Add "Getting Help" section**: Contact information for license support (sales@fluidflowinfo.com, +1 888 711 3051), what information to provide (license key, error messages, FluidFlow version).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword.
Glossary Links (first use only)
NONE — This is an administrative how-to, glossary links not needed.
Internal Links
UP: P01 FluidFlow Product Overview (pillar page, licensing section)
SIDEWAYS: P46 Release History (version compatibility context), P11 Designer Handbook (getting started after license activation)
CTA: Pricing — https://fluidflowinfo.com/pricing/ (for license purchase info)
Selling Message
NONE — This is a technical/administrative guide. Focus on clear instructions, not marketing.
Grounding Tree Branches Used
License types (annual, quarterly, perpetual, named user, trial)
License activation methods (manual, automatic)
Network license installation (from spec sheet)
Global use included
Trial license details (14 full days, online login required, includes full professional features)
Support contact information (sales@fluidflowinfo.com, +1 888 711 3051)
MINI PRD: P51 — Understanding FluidFlow Licensing Options
Status: Plan
Batch: 6 | Priority: P51 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 800-1200
What This Article Does Now

Lists licensing types (Annual, Quarterly, Perpetual, Named User) with basic distinctions. Minimal guidance on selection criteria or real-world use cases. No opening statement, no internal links, no glossary links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the go-to guide for evaluating teams deciding between licensing models. Open with the decision problem ("Which license type fits your team?"), explain each model with clear use cases, cost considerations, and scalability. End with a decision matrix and CTA to Contact Sales or Free Trial.
Specific Changes Required
**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the licensing decision problem, what the reader will understand after reading, and name FluidFlow directly. No preamble.

**Expand each license type section**: Add 1-2 paragraphs per type with real-world use cases (e.g., Annual for multi-site teams, Perpetual for stable long-term users, Named User for individual consultants).
**Add decision matrix table**: Compare license types by team size, budget model, geographic distribution, and upgrade frequency.
**Add "Common Questions" section**: Address typical concerns (network vs. standalone, concurrent users, license portability, upgrade rights).
**Add internal linking structure** (see below).
**Add CTA**: Contact Us (https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact) for licensing questions, Free Trial (https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/) to test before committing.
**Add meta description**: 155-160 chars with primary keyword + benefit.
Glossary Links (first use only)
None applicable for licensing content
Internal Links
UP: P05 FluidFlow Installation & Activation Guide (installation context)
SIDEWAYS: P50 License Manager, P52 Standalone License Transfer Guide, P60 Comprehensive FAQ
DOWN: None (no glossary)
CTA: Contact Us — https://fluidflowinfo.com/contact, Free Trial — https://fluidflowinfo.com/freetrial/
Selling Message
None (licensing is utility content, not a pitch moment)
Grounding Tree Branches Used
License types: Annual, Quarterly, Perpetual, Named User
Pricing: £3,240 – £9,990 per server seat per annum (includes SUM)
Perpetual: Available from 2 seats upwards
Trial: 14 full days, includes full professional features
MINI PRD: P52 — FluidFlow Standalone License Transfer Guide
Status: Plan
Batch: 6 | Priority: P52 | Type: How-to | Word Target: 800-1200
What This Article Does Now

Provides basic steps for transferring standalone license to another machine. Minimal troubleshooting guidance, no visual aids, no context for when/why transfers are needed. No opening statement, no internal links.
What It Should Do After Refactor

Serve as the definitive step-by-step guide for standalone license transfers. Open with the transfer scenario (upgrading hardware, replacing machines, troubleshooting activation issues), provide clear numbered steps, common failure modes, and support escalation path.
Specific Changes Required

**Add opening statement** (2-3 sentences): State the transfer scenario (machine upgrade, hardware failure, license stuck on old PC), what the reader will accomplish, and name FluidFlow directly. No preamble.
